elements.sent-2411-001: The necessary time required to control a many-body quantum system is a critically important issue for the future development of quantum technologies.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, it is generally quite difficult to analyze directly, since the time evolution operator acting on a quantum system is in the form of time-ordered exponential.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work, we examine the Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff (BCH) formula in detail and show that a distance between unitaries can be introduced, allowing us to obtain a lower bound on the control time.
elements.sent-2411-004: We find that, as far as we can compare, this lower bound on control time is tighter (better) than the standard quantum speed limits.
elements.sent-2411-005: This is because this distance takes into account the algebraic structure induced by Hamiltonians through the BCH formula, reflecting the curved nature of operator space.
elements.sent-2411-006: Consequently, we can avoid estimates based on shortcuts through algebraically impossible paths, in contrast to geometric methods that estimate the control time solely by looking at the target state or unitary operator.
elements.sent-2411-007: How long does it take to apply a desired control (unitary transformation) to a many-body quantum system? The biggest obstacle in quantum control is that the time for which quantum coherence can be maintained is severely limited.
elements.sent-2411-008: Meaningful controls must be completed within this rather short coherence time.
elements.sent-2411-009: Therefore, in addition to efforts to extend coherence times, it is crucial to understand how much time is fundamentally required for control.
elements.sent-2411-010: The most well known in this context might be the concept of ``quantum speed limit" (See, e.g., \cite{Frey2016,DC17 , for reviews).}
elements.sent-2411-011: Pioneering work on quantum speed limits include those derived by Mandelstam and Tamm \cite{MT85 , and Margolus and Levitin \cite{MargolusLevitin1998 , which attempted to estimate the time it takes for an initial state to evolve into a final state under a given Hamiltonian.}}
elements.sent-2411-012: Roughly speaking, these approaches evaluate the geometrical ``distance" between the initial and final states and relate it to the speed of evolution.
elements.sent-2411-013: Since then, there have been a number of work that tackled the problem of speed limits/optimal controls from the geometrical points of view with variational principle \cite{Carlini2006,Carlini2007,BSS2021,Koike2022,poggi2019,poggi2022 , as well as endeavors to obtain analytical forms of optimal control sequences for some specific classes of systems\cite{Khaneja2001,Khaneja2002,Caneva2009,Chau2010,Lee2012,Bussell2015,Sevitz2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Further, there have recently been attempts to derive lower bounds on the number of quantum gates or the control time with various Hamiltonians, based on inequalities that relate the distance between unitary transformations (the norm of difference) to the corresponding Hamiltonians \cite{NielsenScience2006,NielsenPRA2006,Dowling2008,Arenz2017,Lee2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: The complexity and difficulty of the problem of control times stem from the noncommutativity between Hamiltonians, i.e., between the (infinitesimal) generators of unitary transformations.
elements.sent-2411-016: Noncommutativity is the source of the rich dynamics of quantum systems and their controllability: The number of generators (driving Hamiltonians) corresponding to the directly controllable fields is typically much smaller than the dimension of the quantum system.
elements.sent-2411-017: Even so, in many cases, the noncommuting Hamiltonians are capable of generating a number of new independent generators that can span an effective algebra of sufficiently high dimension \cite{dalessandroBook,Lloyd2004,bmm2010,bmBookChapter .}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, evaluating the time optimality of quantum control with just a few noncommutative generators is extremely hard.
elements.sent-2411-019: To the best of authors' knowledge, there are very few studies in which global properties, such as the execution time of the target unitary transformation, have been derived from local properties, i.e., the noncommutativity, of the generators.
elements.sent-2411-020: A few ambitious examples include a study that has rigorously achieved this for two-dimensional systems \cite{boozer2012 , and the one that explicitly handles algebraic structures by approximating Hamiltonians linearly over short time intervals \cite{poggi2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Our principal tool here is the renowned Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff (BCH) formula.
elements.sent-2411-022: It demonstrates a constructive way to express a single generator $C$, which achieves the same group operation as applying two generators $A$ and $B$ sequentially, namely, $e^{C =e^{B e^{A}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-023: Yet, this generator $C$ has a form of infinite series, and the series does not converge unless the norms of $A$ and $B$ are small.
elements.sent-2411-024: Therefore, it is valid only in the vicinity of identity operation.
elements.sent-2411-025: Nevertheless, the BCH formula is suitable for quantitatively analyzing the time optimality of quantum operations achievable.
elements.sent-2411-026: In this paper, we attempt to derive a lower bound on the control time, taking into account the algebra $\mathcal{L}$ obtainable through the noncommutativity of driving Hamiltonians.
elements.sent-2411-027: By applying the BCH formula carefully, we show that the distance in the global space of quantum operations can be introduced with local generators.
elements.sent-2411-028: More specifically, for a control sequence $H(t)$ that realizes the target unitary transformation $U^\mathrm{(target)}$, we show that a single anti-Hermitian generator $C$, which could achieve $U^\mathrm{(target) =e^{C}$, is included in $\mathcal{L}$, and that a relationship analogous to the triangle inequality holds between these generators.}
elements.sent-2411-029: This relationship serves as a formula for the lower bound on the control time required to execute $U^\mathrm{(target)}$ using $H(t)$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Considering the effective algebra $\mathcal{L}$, we can avoid underestimating the lower bound due to infeasible ``shortcuts" that might occur when only looking at the target unitary $U^\mathrm{(target)}$.
elements.sent-2411-031: As far as we can compare, our lower bound provides a tighter (better) one than previous estimates.

elements.sent-2411-034: Since we are interested in the time length that is necessary to achieve a desired quantum operation, we shall base our discussion on the Schr\"{o dinger equation, as well as an initial condition, in the operator form: \begin{equation}\label{schroedinger} i\frac{d}{dt}U(t) = H(t) U(t), \label{schroedinger_ic} U(0) = I,\end{equation} where $U(t)$ is a unitary operator that represents the quantum operation on a state, $H(t)$ is the time-dependent Hamiltonian, and $I$ is the identity operator.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Also, the Planck constant is set to $\hbar =1$.
elements.sent-2411-036: In the context of quantum control, we often assume that there are a (relatively small) number of control Hamiltonians, $\{H_m|m\in\{1,2,...,M\} \}$, where $M$ is the number of directly controllable fields by apparatus, e.g., electromagnetic fields applied on the system.
elements.sent-2411-037: We shall let $\{H_m\}$ denote this set for short in what follows, and measurements are not explicitly involved in our present scenario as a control means.
elements.sent-2411-038: Thus $H(t)$ in Eq. (\ref{schroedinger ) has a general form \begin{equation}\label{hamt} H(t)=\sum_{m=0}^M h_m(t) H_m,\end{equation} where $h_m(t)$ are the modulation of controllable fields.}
elements.sent-2411-039: There is often a Hamiltonian that is not subject to artificial control, e.g., the one that describes the internal system interaction.

elements.sent-2411-044: Throughout this paper, generators that form an algebra are assumed to be Hermitian, only when they are Hamiltonians in the context of quantum dynamics, while they are considered to be anti-Hermitian operators otherwise, namely for general discussions on Lie alegbra.
elements.sent-2411-045: We shall make this distinction as clear as possible by specifying it in words and also putting $i(=\sqrt{-1 )$ in front of Hamiltonians.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Although the number $M$ of such Hamiltonians is usually much smaller than the dimension $D$ of the quantum system, it is in general possible to effectively realize more Hamiltonians that are obtained from $\{i H_m\}$ through Lie bracket, i.e., commutator.
elements.sent-2411-047: That is, if thereby obtained Hamiltonians can span the full Lie algebra and generate any unitary operator on the Hilbert space of the quantum system, the system is fully controllable.
elements.sent-2411-048: This set of (anti-Hermitian) Hamiltonians is called the dynamical Lie algebra $\mathcal{L (\{i H_m\})$ \cite{dalessandroBook,Lloyd2004,bmm2010,bmBookChapter .}}

elements.section-2411-003: Comparison with quantum speed limits for state transforms
elements.sent-2411-088: Let us now discuss how our bound, which will be denoted by $T_*$, can be compared with those implied by well-known discussions on quantum speed limits.
elements.sent-2411-089: Two relevant bounds may be the one by Mandelstam and Tamm \cite{MT85 and the other by Margolus and Levitin \cite{MargolusLevitin1998 (See also reviews \cite{Frey2016,DC17 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: The Mandelstam-Tamm (MT) bound is based on the uncertainty relation between energy and time, and the Margolus-Levitin (ML) is obtained from the unitary evolution of state.
elements.sent-2411-091: There is a subtle difference in the formulations of their bounds and ours: While the MT and ML bounds measure the evolution speed in terms of the fidelity between the initial and final (target) states, we consider the time to achieve a target unitary operation $U^\mathrm{(target)}$ since our main interest is in the implementation of quantum control operations, rather than reaching a specific fidelity with respect to the initial state.
elements.sent-2411-092: Thus, although the MT and ML bounds are measures of use in their own right, it makes little sense in making direct comparison between theirs and ours.
elements.sent-2411-093: Nevertheless, it is possible to connect them through the Choi representation of quantum operation, i.e., by translating the language of operations into that of states, our bound may be seen stronger than those bounds as we shall show below.
elements.sent-2411-094: Before going into the discussion of comparison, let us remind ourselves of the Mandelstamm-Tamm's and Margolus-Levitin's statements.
elements.sent-2411-095: Since we here focus on unitary operations on pure states, suppose that the dynamics of a state vector $\ket{\phi(t)}$ is described by the Schr\"odinger equation \begin{equation}\label{sch_MT} i\frac d{dt}\ket{\phi (t)}=H(t)\ket{\phi(t)},\end{equation} where $H(t)$ is a time-dependent Hamiltonian, thus a Hermitian operator.
elements.sent-2411-096: Also, let $\ket{\phi(0)}$ and $\ket{\phi(\tau)}$ be the initial and target states, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-097: Then, they can be neatly summarized as follows.
